Category: --- OBSERVATIONS: site findings documented during construction — the earliest quality and safety signal ---

Description: A documented finding from a site walk or inspection — captures conditions, hazards, deficiencies, or noteworthy items during active construction. The primary during-construction quality and safety signal. Key properties: name (issue description, 0% null, avg 33 chars), category (structured: Quality, Safety, Environmental, Commissioning, Warranty, Work to Complete — 0% null), type_name (severity/workflow: Corrective Action, Deficiency, Non-Conformance, Safety Hazard, Safety Violation — NOT issue type), status (Open, In Progress, Ready for Review, Closed), assignee (responsible party via vendor_id, 80% populated), location. Observations are LEADING indicators — 63% correlation with downstream rework, 28-day lead time, 1.98x multiplier. Lifecycle: Created → Assigned → In Progress → Ready for Review → Closed.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (safety); ISO 9001 §8.7 (nonconformity); company-specific observation programs
Common Values: Quality, Safety, Environmental, Commissioning, Warranty, Work to Complete; severity via type_name
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; Fieldwire; PlanGrid; BIM 360 Field; paper forms; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — observation table with structured category, type_name, status, hazard_name, vendor_id. 5,923+ cos with quality observations. Widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Compare observation density (per $M or per SF) by category and project type to benchmark quality/safety culture
Notes: observation.name is the classification field for keyword clustering (18 validated issue categories). type_name is severity, NOT issue type. trade_id exists but has NO lookup table — dead end for V1. vendor_id (80% populated) is the trade attribution path.

Description: An observation with category = 'Safety' — documents hazards, unsafe conditions, and safety compliance findings during site walks. Key addition: hazard_name field provides OSHA-aligned structured taxonomy (Fall, Struck By, Electrical, Caught In/Between, etc.) — 55% populated on safety observations. 16 hazard types with 50+ companies each. Recurrence detection: same hazard × project × week; 2+ consecutive weeks = 'recurring', 3+ = 'systemic'. Recurrence rates range 29–45% by hazard type.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety); OSHA Focus Four Hazards; ANSI/ASSP Z590.3
Common Values: Fall Protection, Struck By, Electrical, Caught In/Between, Housekeeping, PPE, Scaffolding, Excavation, Ladder Safety
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; Safety management platforms; paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — observation table with hazard_name (OSHA-aligned taxonomy), 1,296+ cos with recurring safety patterns
Benchmarking Use: Compare hazard recurrence rates and safety observation density across project types and companies
Notes: hazard_name is 45.4% null — only 55% of safety obs have structured hazards. Corrective action linkage is sparse (1.2% via incident_action). Daily log safety violations were validated as UNUSABLE for recurrence tracking (subject 66% null, no vendor FK).

Description: An observation with category = 'Quality' — documents workmanship deficiencies, material issues, and quality non-conformances during active construction. The strongest during-construction quality signal. keyword clustering on observation.name yields 18 issue categories including Doors/Windows, Electrical, Paint/Finish, Plumbing/Piping, Cracks/Damage, HVAC, Waterproofing. 43% of quality observations cluster into named categories. 3D analysis validated: issue distribution varies by project type (Commercial = Doors/Windows #1, Healthcare = Electrical #1, Industrial = Cracks/Damage #1).
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 §8.7 (Control of Nonconforming Outputs); company-specific QC programs
Common Values: Corrective Action, Deficiency, Non-Conformance; 18 issue categories via keyword clustering
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; Fieldwire; BIM 360 Field; paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — observation table filtered by category = 'Quality'. 5,923 cos with quality observation data.
Benchmarking Use: Compare quality observation density and issue category distribution across project types
Notes: Quality observations + punch items form the dual-signal model for Common Quality Issues (insight #4). Observations are during-construction; punch items are closeout. Combined: 12,556 unique companies.

Description: Observations with category = 'Environmental' or 'Commissioning' — documents environmental compliance findings (erosion control, dust, spills) and commissioning findings (system performance, functional testing results, equipment issues). Lower volume than Quality and Safety categories but important for specialized compliance and handover tracking.
Industry Standard: EPA NPDES (environmental); ASHRAE Guideline 0 (commissioning); LEED commissioning prerequisites
Common Values: Environmental: erosion, sediment, spill, dust, dewatering. Commissioning: functional test, performance, equipment startup, controls verification.
Source Systems: Procore; specialized commissioning software (Cx Alloy, Kahua); environmental compliance tools; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — same observation table, lower adoption for Environmental and Commissioning categories compared to Quality/Safety
Benchmarking Use: Compare commissioning observation volume per system to benchmark handover quality
Notes: Environmental and Commissioning observations follow the same data structure but serve different compliance purposes. Commissioning observations often link to specific building systems (HVAC, electrical, controls).

Category: --- INSPECTIONS: formal examinations against checklists or standards ---

Description: A formal structured examination of work against defined criteria — conducted by quality managers, safety officers, third-party inspectors, or regulatory authorities. Key properties: name, status (Open, In Progress, Ready for Review, Closed), type (company-defined), inspection_type (Safety, Quality, Commissioning — but 93% null on group field, nearly useless). Inspections contain inspection items (individual checklist lines). Scheduled inspections track recurring compliance checks. Lifecycle: Created/Scheduled → In Progress → Complete → Closed.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 §8.6 (Release of Products); OSHA inspection requirements; IBC special inspections; ACI 318 (concrete testing)
Common Values: Safety, Quality, Commissioning, Pre-pour, Pre-cover, Structural, Electrical, Fire Protection, Environmental
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; Fieldwire; PlanGrid; paper checklists; BIM 360 Field
Procore Coverage: High — inspections table with status, type, result tracking. inspection_assignees, inspection_schedules, inspection_item_activity, inspection_item_comments all READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare inspection volume and pass rate across project types and inspection categories
Notes: inspections.group values are lowercase ('quality', 'safety') but 93% null — nearly useless for categorization. Inspection type is the better filter. Inspections are the formal complement to observations — observations are ad-hoc, inspections are scheduled.

Description: An individual line item within an inspection checklist — the atomic unit of conformance checking. Key properties: name (item description), status (structured, capitalized: 'Conforming', 'Deficient', 'Not Applicable', 'Uninspected', 'Neutral'). Deficient items trigger corrective action workflows. Deficiency rate ranges 2–6% by inspection type across 5,470 companies. Names are safety-focused and more descriptive than observation names.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 checklist requirements; OSHA checklist standards; company-specific inspection templates
Common Values: Conforming, Deficient, Not Applicable, Uninspected, Neutral; 91K+ recurring deficiency patterns across 1,630 cos
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; paper checklists
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_items table with structured status values. inspection_item_activity and inspection_item_comments provide audit trail.
Benchmarking Use: Compare deficiency rate (Deficient / Total Items) across inspection types and project types
Notes: inspection_items.status values are CAPITALIZED ('Deficient' not 'deficient'). Recurring deficiency detection: same item name × project × inspection type. Deficient items in inspections are a second signal for violation tracking (insight #11).

Description: A recurring or planned inspection defined in advance — ensures systematic compliance checking on a regular cadence. Tracks inspection frequency, assigned inspector, and schedule compliance. Scheduled inspections that are missed or late are a compliance gap signal.
Industry Standard: OSHA periodic inspection requirements; IBC §1704 (special inspections); contract-specified inspection schedules
Common Values: Daily safety walk, weekly quality audit, monthly fire protection check, pre-pour inspection, pre-cover inspection, close-in inspection
Source Systems: Procore; iAuditor/SafetyCulture; scheduling tools; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_schedules table tracks planned inspections with frequency and assignee
Benchmarking Use: Compare scheduled vs. actual inspection compliance rate across project types
Notes: Scheduled inspection adherence rate = actual inspections / scheduled inspections. Low adherence signals resource constraints or deprioritized quality/safety programs.

Description: An inspection conducted by an independent testing or inspection agency — required by building codes for structural, fire protection, and other critical systems. Special inspections are mandated by IBC Chapter 17 for specific construction activities (structural steel, concrete placement, fireproofing, sprinkler systems). Results are reported to the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ). Distinct from internal QC inspections — third-party inspections carry regulatory weight.
Industry Standard: IBC §1704 (Special Inspections); ACI 318; AISC 360; NFPA 13; local jurisdiction requirements
Common Values: Structural steel (welding, bolting), concrete (pre-pour, placement, curing), fireproofing, sprinkler systems, soil compaction, waterproofing
Source Systems: Procore (as inspections or forms); testing lab portals; paper reports; specialized inspection software
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked through inspections tool or forms, but no dedicated third-party inspection table. Results often uploaded as attachments rather than structured data.
Benchmarking Use: Compare third-party inspection pass rate and required retest frequency across project types
Notes: Third-party inspection failures often trigger re-work and schedule delays. Results connect to building elements (which system was inspected) and financial instruments (cost of retesting). Testing agency is an Organization domain entity.

Category: --- PUNCH ITEMS: deficiencies requiring correction before project acceptance ---

Description: A documented deficiency or incomplete work item that must be corrected before the owner accepts the project — compiled during substantial completion walkthrough. The strongest closeout quality signal. Key properties: name (issue description, 0% null, avg 33 chars), punch_item_type_name (severity/priority — NOT issue type), workflow_status (Open, In Progress, Ready for Review, Closed, Not Accepted), assignee (responsible vendor via vendor_id), location, due_date. Keyword clustering on name yields 18 issue categories — Paint/Finish is #1 universally (closeout cosmetic), but #2–#4 vary by project type. 11,722 companies with punch item data. Lifecycle: Created → Assigned → In Progress → Ready for Review → Closed.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §9.8 (Substantial Completion); AIA G704 (Certificate of Substantial Completion); contract-specific punchlist provisions
Common Values: Open, In Progress, Ready for Review, Closed, Not Accepted; 18 issue categories via keyword clustering on name field
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid (Autodesk); Fieldwire; BIM 360 Field; paper punchlists; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — punch_item table with structured workflow_status, vendor assignment, location. 11,722+ cos. Massive adoption.
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item density (items per unit or per SF) and closure rate across project types and value bands
Notes: punch_item.name is the classification field (0% null). punch_item_type_name is severity/priority, NOT issue category. Punch items are TRAILING indicators — they appear 17 days AFTER the underlying issue. Combined with observations: 12,556 unique companies in dual-signal model (insight #4).

Description: A categorized view of punch items grouped by the 18 validated issue categories derived from keyword clustering on name field. Categories: Paint/Finish, Doors/Windows, Plumbing/Piping, Electrical, HVAC, Caulk/Sealant, Hardware, Drywall/Plaster, Flooring, Fire Protection, Cracks/Damage, Concrete, Roofing, Waterproofing, Insulation, Elevator, Glass/Glazing, Cleanup. Distribution varies by project type — this is the '3D hot sheet' for quality issue prioritization.
Industry Standard: No industry standard for issue categorization; keyword taxonomy validated against Procore data
Common Values: Paint/Finish (#1 everywhere), Doors/Windows, Electrical, Plumbing/Piping, HVAC, Caulk/Sealant, Hardware, Drywall — top 8 cover ~60% of clustered items
Source Systems: Derived from: punch_item.name keyword clustering
Procore Coverage: High — derived from structured punch_item.name field via keyword extraction
Benchmarking Use: Compare issue category distribution across project types to identify type-specific quality risks
Notes: 7 project types with 99–1,358 cos for punch items. 74% of punch items cluster into named categories. This is the core of insight #4 (Common Quality Issues).

Description: The lifecycle tracking of a punch item from creation to closure — measures how quickly deficiencies are corrected and by whom. Key properties: workflow_status transitions (with timestamps), assignee response, resolution description, verification. Resolution time = Closed Date − Created Date. Resolution involves: vendor fixes deficiency → PM verifies → owner/architect accepts. Not Accepted items require rework and restart the cycle.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §9.8; contract-specific punchlist closure requirements; typical 30-day closure window
Common Values: Resolution time typical: 7–30 days for individual items; 30–90 days for full punchlist closure
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; Fieldwire; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — punch_item workflow_status transitions with timestamps enable resolution tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item resolution time by trade and issue category to benchmark closeout efficiency
Notes: Resolution time by vendor reveals which subcontractors are responsive to punchlist items. Prolonged punch items block certificate of substantial completion and retainage release. Cross-domain: links to Financial Instruments (retainage) and Phases (closeout).

Category: --- INCIDENTS: safety events requiring formal documentation and reporting ---

Description: A safety event that results in injury, illness, property damage, near-miss, or environmental release — the formal record of what happened. Key properties: title, status (Draft, Open, Closed), description, date/time, location, severity, recordable (OSHA classification). Incidents trigger investigation, corrective actions, and regulatory reporting. Lifecycle: Reported → Under Investigation → Corrective Actions Assigned → Closed. More severe than observations — incidents are reactive (something happened), observations are proactive (something could happen).
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904 (Recordkeeping); OSHA 300/300A/301 logs; ANSI Z16.1 (Injury Classification)
Common Values: Near Miss, First Aid, Medical Treatment, Restricted Duty, Lost Time, Fatality; Property Damage, Environmental Release
Source Systems: Procore; Intelex; VelocityEHS; iSight; company safety databases; OSHA logs (paper/electronic)
Procore Coverage: High — incident table with structured title, status, description. incident_alert for notifications. All tables READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare incident frequency (per 200K hours) and severity distribution across project types and companies
Notes: Incidents are the most serious safety signal. OSHA-recordable incidents drive TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate). Near-miss reporting is a leading indicator — high near-miss-to-incident ratio indicates strong safety culture.

Description: A detailed record within an incident — captures specific injury details, property damage details, or environmental release information. One incident may have multiple records (e.g., two people injured in the same event). Key properties: record type (injury, damage, cost), involved person details, body part, nature of injury, cause, medical treatment, days away/restricted. The OSHA 301 equivalent.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904.7 (General Recording Criteria); OSHA Form 301 (Injury and Illness Incident Report)
Common Values: Injury (body part, nature, cause), Property Damage (item, extent, cost), Environmental (release type, material, volume)
Source Systems: Procore; VelocityEHS; Intelex; OSHA electronic reporting
Procore Coverage: High — incident_record table with detailed entries for injury, damage, cost. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare injury type distribution (body part
Notes: nature) across trades and project types
column10: incident_record provides the granular data needed for OSHA 300 log compilation. Injury body part and nature fields enable pattern detection — fall-related hand/wrist injuries in steel erection, back injuries in concrete. Links to Organizations via involved company.

Description: A documented account from a person who witnessed a safety event — captured as part of the incident investigation. Key properties: witness identity, statement text, date. Witness statements support root cause analysis and regulatory compliance. Required for OSHA investigations and insurance claims.
Industry Standard: OSHA investigation requirements; company safety investigation procedures
Common Values: Free-text statement with witness identification, date, and signature
Source Systems: Procore; paper forms; investigation software
Procore Coverage: High — incident_witness_statement table. READY.
Benchmarking Use: N/A — witness statements are qualitative records not suited to quantitative benchmarking
Notes: Witness statements support incident investigation but are free-text — no structured data for analytics. Volume of witness statements per incident may indicate investigation thoroughness.

Description: A required remediation or preventive measure assigned in response to an incident or recurring safety observation. Key properties: action description, assignee, due date, status (Open, In Progress, Complete), linked incident or observation. Corrective actions close the loop between identifying a problem and fixing it. Lifecycle: Assigned → In Progress → Complete → Verified.
Industry Standard: ISO 45001 §10.2 (Incident Investigation and Corrective Action); OSHA corrective action requirements; ANSI Z10
Common Values: Immediate corrective action, Root cause corrective action, Preventive action, Training, Engineering control, Administrative control
Source Systems: Procore; Intelex; VelocityEHS; iSight; safety management platforms
Procore Coverage: High — incident_action table with structured action tracking. READY. However, linkage to observations is sparse — only 1.2% of recurring observations have linked incident_actions.
Benchmarking Use: Compare corrective action closure rate and time-to-close across incident types and companies
Notes: Corrective action completion rate is a key safety program effectiveness metric. Sparse linkage to observations (1.2%) means most corrective actions flow through the incident pathway, not the observation pathway. Cross-domain: may trigger financial instruments (cost of corrective measures).

Category: --- FORMAL NOTICES & REPORTS: documented deviations, violations, and non-conformances ---

Description: WARNING: daily_log_safety_violation is UNUSABLE for analytics — subject 66% null, vendor_name is free text (no FK). Use observation.category = 'Safety' with hazard_name for structured violation tracking instead. Formal regulatory violation record — OSHA citation, code violation, or safety rule infraction. More formal than observation — implies specific regulation breached. Key properties: violation type, regulation cited, responsible party, corrective action, abatement date.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety Standards); state OSHA plans; local safety ordinances
Common Values: OSHA Serious, OSHA Other-than-Serious, OSHA Willful, OSHA Repeat; company-specific violation categories
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_safety_violation); safety management platforms; OSHA citation records; paper forms
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_safety_violation table exists but subject is 66% null, vendor_name is free text (no FK). Usable for volume tracking only, not structured analysis.
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety violation frequency per 200K hours across project types
Notes: Daily log safety violations validated as UNUSABLE for recurrence tracking (insight #11) — too sparse and unstructured. For structured violation tracking, use observation.category = 'Safety' with hazard_name instead.

Description: A formal documented deviation from specifications, drawings, or quality standards — triggers investigation and disposition (accept as-is, rework, reject/replace). More rigorous than an observation or deficiency — NCRs require root cause analysis and formal disposition authority (typically architect or engineer of record). Key properties: description, specification reference, drawing reference, root cause, disposition, responsible party.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 §8.7 (Control of Nonconforming Outputs); ISO 19011 (audit); company QC procedures
Common Values: Accept as-is, Rework, Reject and Replace, Use as-is with concession; root cause categories
Source Systems: Procore (as observations with type_name = 'Non-Conformance' or as forms); NCR tracking software; Excel; paper forms
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked through observation table (type_name = 'Non-Conformance') or custom forms. No dedicated NCR table, but the observation structure captures the core workflow.
Benchmarking Use: Compare NCR frequency by building system and trade to identify quality problem areas
Notes: NCRs bridge quality records to financial instruments — rework disposition drives cost (rework vs. replacement). NCR frequency by specification section identifies problematic design details. Cross-domain: references specific drawings and specification sections.

Description: A formal written notification to a subcontractor or vendor that their work does not meet contract requirements — triggers contractual obligations to correct. More formal than an observation — a deficiency notice is a contractual instrument with potential financial consequences (backcharges, liquidated damages). Key properties: description, contract reference, specification reference, cure period, financial consequences.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §12.2 (Correction of Work); contract-specific cure provisions
Common Values: Written notice, Cure period (typically 7–14 days), Backcharge if uncured, Contract termination for repeated deficiencies
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence or form); contract management systems; paper letters
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated deficiency notice table. Tracked as correspondence, observations, or letters. The formal contractual weight is in the document, not the tracking system.
Benchmarking Use: Compare deficiency notice frequency by trade to identify subcontractor performance patterns
Notes: Deficiency notices connect quality records to financial instruments (backcharges) and organizations (vendor performance). High deficiency notice frequency for a vendor across projects signals systemic quality issues.

Category: --- COORDINATION ISSUES: design and constructability problems identified during coordination ---

Description: A design or constructability problem identified during BIM coordination, drawing review, or field coordination — captures clashes, design conflicts, and constructability concerns before they become field problems. Key properties: issue_type (structured: Clash, Coordination, Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change, Existing Condition, Client Feedback, Building Code, Other), trade (free text — NOT standardized), status, priority, rfi_header_id (links to RFIs, 4,967 linked issues across 204 cos). Lifecycle: Identified → Under Review → Resolved → Closed.
Industry Standard: BIM Execution Plan requirements; AGC BIMForum Level of Development; company coordination procedures
Common Values: Clash, Coordination, Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change, Existing Condition, Client Feedback, Building Code
Source Systems: Procore; Navisworks; Solibri; BIM 360 Glue; BIM Track; dRofus; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — coordination_issue table with structured issue_type, status, priority. rfi_header_id provides direct linkage to RFIs. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare coordination issue density by issue_type and discipline across project types
Notes: coordination_issue.trade is free text, NOT standardized ('Plumbing' vs '22 Plumbing' vs '22 00 00 Plumbing'). MEP-heavy. 17,999 design-typed issues (Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change) across 327 cos. Links to RFIs and change events for cost impact tracing.

Description: Coordination issues with issue_type in (Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change) — the subset that signals design quality and completeness problems. These issues often become RFIs (via rfi_header_id link) and ultimately change events with cost impact. Part of the design-related change impact tracing path (insight #31): Coordination Issue → RFI → Change Event → Cost.
Industry Standard: AGC BIMForum; AIA E203 (BIM Protocol); design coordination meeting protocols
Common Values: Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change; trade distribution: MEP-dominated
Source Systems: Derived from: coordination_issue filtered by issue_type
Procore Coverage: High — structured issue_type field, direct RFI linkage via rfi_header_id
Benchmarking Use: Compare design-related coordination issue volume across project types to benchmark design completeness
Notes: 4,967 coordination issues linked to RFIs via rfi_header_id across 204 cos. Design-typed coordination issues are a V2 signal for insight #31 (Design-Related Change Impact). Cross-domain: links to Documents (RFIs), Financial Instruments (change events), and Building Elements (affected systems).

Category: --- TEST REPORTS: material and system testing results ---

Description: Results of material testing required by specifications and building codes — concrete cylinder breaks, soil compaction tests, steel mill certifications, fireproofing adhesion tests, waterproofing tests. Conducted by independent testing laboratories. Pass/fail results directly affect whether construction can proceed. Key properties: test type, material, specification reference, result (pass/fail/value), tested by (lab), date.
Industry Standard: ASTM test standards (C39 concrete, D1557 soil, E119 fire); ACI 318; AISC; IBC §1704
Common Values: Concrete compressive strength, Soil compaction (Proctor), Steel tensile strength, Fireproofing thickness, Air/water infiltration, Welding inspection
Source Systems: Testing laboratory portals; Procore (as forms or documents); paper reports; LIMS software
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated test report table. Results uploaded as attachments or tracked through forms/inspections. Testing data is highly structured at the lab but loses structure when entered into project management systems.
Benchmarking Use: Compare test failure rate by material type and project type to benchmark quality control effectiveness
Notes: Material test failures trigger rework (re-pour concrete, re-compact soil, replace steel). Test report data connects quality records to building elements (which system was tested) and financial instruments (cost of retest and rework). Testing labs are Organization domain entities.

Description: Results of functional testing for building systems — HVAC air balance, electrical load testing, fire alarm testing, plumbing pressure tests, elevator load tests, generator load bank tests. Conducted during commissioning phase. Key properties: system tested, test procedure, acceptance criteria, actual results, pass/fail, deficiencies noted, retest requirements.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE standards (HVAC); NFPA 72 (fire alarm), NFPA 25 (sprinkler); NEC (electrical); ASME A17.1 (elevator); manufacturer-specific procedures
Common Values: HVAC air balance, Electrical load test, Fire alarm test, Plumbing pressure test, Elevator load test, Generator load bank, Building envelope air/water
Source Systems: Procore (as inspections or forms); commissioning software (Cx Alloy); TAB reports; paper test reports
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked through inspections tool or commissioning forms. No dedicated system test table. Test results are typically uploaded as documents rather than structured data.
Benchmarking Use: Compare system test pass rate and retest frequency across project types to benchmark commissioning quality
Notes: System performance tests are the quality gate for building system handover. Failed tests trigger commissioning observations and corrective actions. Cross-domain: directly linked to Building Elements (which system), Phases (commissioning), and Organizations (commissioning agent, TAB contractor).

Category: --- ACTION PLANS: structured remediation and task tracking linked to quality/safety findings ---

Description: A structured set of tasks created in response to quality or safety findings — groups related corrective actions into a trackable plan with assignees, due dates, and approval workflows. Key properties: title, status, assignees, due dates, approval workflow. Action plans aggregate individual tasks (line items) that may reference observations, punch items, inspections, or incidents as source records. Lifecycle: Created → In Progress → Under Review → Approved → Closed.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 §10.1 (Improvement); ISO 45001 §10.2; company-specific corrective action procedures
Common Values: Quality improvement plan, Safety corrective action plan, Environmental remediation plan, Commissioning deficiency plan
Source Systems: Procore; corrective action tracking software; Excel; project management tools
Procore Coverage: High — action_plan table with structured status and workflow. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare action plan closure rate and cycle time across project types and categories
Notes: Action plans are the management response layer — they organize individual findings into structured remediation. Cross-domain: action plan tasks reference source records across observations, inspections, incidents, and punch items.

Description: An individual task within an action plan — the atomic unit of remediation work. Key properties: description, assignee, due date, status, linked record (the source observation, punch item, or incident that triggered this task). The action_plan_line_item_record linkage connects tasks to their source issues, creating traceability from finding to resolution.
Industry Standard: Follows parent action plan standard/format
Common Values: Task description, assignee, due date, status (Open, In Progress, Complete), priority, linked source record
Source Systems: Procore; task management tools; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — action_plan_line_item table with structured task tracking. action_plan_line_item_record provides cross-reference to source issues. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare task completion rate and overdue task percentage across action plan types
Notes: action_plan_line_item_record is the key linkage table — it connects remediation tasks back to the specific observation, punch item, or incident that triggered the action. This creates the full quality/safety lifecycle: Finding → Action Plan → Task → Resolution.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from quality and safety record performance ---

Description: Total observations per unit of project size — measures the intensity of quality and safety monitoring. Formula: Total Observations / Project Value (per $M) or Total Observations / Project SF. Can be segmented by category (Quality vs. Safety). High observation density indicates proactive monitoring culture, not necessarily more problems.
Industry Standard: Company-specific safety observation targets; industry benchmarks
Common Values: Typical: varies widely; 50–500+ observations per project depending on size and safety culture
Source Systems: Derived from: observation (count by category), projects (total_value or square_footage)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from observation and project tables
Benchmarking Use: Compare observation density across project types and companies to benchmark safety/quality monitoring intensity
Notes: Higher observation density correlates with better safety outcomes — proactive identification prevents incidents. Companies with < 10 observations per $M may have underreporting.

Description: Percentage of inspection items found deficient — measures quality conformance. Formula: Deficient Items / Total Inspected Items × 100. Can be segmented by inspection type, building system, and trade. Deficiency rate 2–6% is typical across 5,470 companies.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 quality metrics; company QC program benchmarks
Common Values: Typical: 2–6% deficiency rate by inspection type; > 10% indicates systemic quality problems
Source Systems: Derived from: inspection_items (status = 'Deficient' / total items)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from structured inspection_items.status field
Benchmarking Use: Compare deficiency rate across inspection types and project types to identify quality risk areas
Notes: inspection_items.status values are CAPITALIZED. Deficiency rate by trade (via responsible_contractor_id, 48% populated) reveals which subcontractors have quality issues.

Description: Total punch items per unit of project size at substantial completion — measures closeout quality. Formula: Punch Items / Gross SF or Punch Items / Unit Count. Lower density = cleaner handover. Can be segmented by issue category (18 categories) and responsible trade.
Industry Standard: Industry closeout benchmarks; AIA substantial completion criteria
Common Values: Typical: 1–5 punch items per 1,000 SF depending on project type and quality standards
Source Systems: Derived from: punch_item (count), projects (square_footage or unit_count)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from punch_item table with massive adoption (11,722 cos)
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item density across project types and value bands to benchmark closeout quality
Notes: Punch item density is the most widely available quality outcome metric in Procore due to massive adoption. Paint/Finish items inflate density — consider excluding cosmetic items for structural quality benchmarks.

Description: The number of OSHA-recordable incidents per 200,000 worker hours — the standard safety performance metric in construction. Formula: (Recordable Incidents × 200,000) / Total Worker Hours. Recordable = medical treatment beyond first aid, restricted duty, days away from work, fatality. The primary safety benchmark used by owners for prequalification.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904; BLS industry averages; EMR calculation methodology
Common Values: Construction industry average: ~3.0; best-in-class: < 1.0; OSHA uses 200K hours (≈ 100 FTE-years)
Source Systems: Derived from: incident + incident_record (recordable count), daily_log_manpower (worker hours)
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires combining incident data with workforce hours from daily logs. Both tables exist but linkage requires computation.
Benchmarking Use: Compare TRIR across project types and company sizes to benchmark safety performance
Notes: TRIR depends on accurate worker hour tracking (daily_log_manpower) and incident classification. Companies that underreport incidents or overcount hours distort TRIR. Cross-validate with observation density — low TRIR + low observation density = possible underreporting.

Description: Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred rate — incidents per 200,000 hours that result in days away from work, restricted duty, or job transfer. A subset of TRIR that measures more severe incidents. Formula: (DART Incidents × 200,000) / Total Worker Hours. More meaningful than TRIR because it focuses on incidents with actual impact.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904; BLS industry DART averages
Common Values: Construction industry average: ~1.5; best-in-class: < 0.5
Source Systems: Derived from: incident_record (days_away, restricted_duty flags), daily_log_manpower (hours)
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires incident severity classification from incident_record details
Benchmarking Use: Compare DART rate across project types and company sizes
Notes: DART rate is a better predictor of safety program effectiveness than TRIR because it excludes minor first-aid incidents. Owner prequalification increasingly uses DART rate alongside TRIR.

Description: Percentage of identified hazards that recur within the same project — measures safety program effectiveness at eliminating root causes. Formula: Recurring Hazards / Total Unique Hazards × 100. A recurring hazard = same hazard_name appearing in 2+ consecutive weeks on the same project. 3+ consecutive weeks = systemic.
Industry Standard: ISO 45001 continual improvement metrics; company safety program KPIs
Common Values: Typical: 29–45% recurrence rate by hazard type across 1,296 cos
Source Systems: Derived from: observation (category = 'Safety', hazard_name, date, project) with LAG window function
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires consecutive-week detection via window functions on observation data
Benchmarking Use: Compare hazard recurrence rate by hazard type and project type to benchmark safety program effectiveness
Notes: Recurrence detection uses 14-day gap tolerance between consecutive observations. Fall Protection has highest volume; Infrastructure projects average 14.4 weeks of recurring hazards. From insight #11 (Recurring Violation Tracking).

Description: Rework-attributable cost as a percentage of total project cost — the financial impact of quality failures. Formula: Rework Cost / Total Project Cost × 100. Rework cost sourced from four signals: backcharges (CE/CCO with rework change_reason, deduplicated via GREATEST), direct costs (WBS-coded rework), budget modifications (to rework-coded lines), and observation/punch item linkage. Conservative total: ~$4.8B structured; with NLP: ~$5.8B.
Industry Standard: CII (Construction Industry Institute) rework benchmarks; typical 5–15% of project cost
Common Values: Industry benchmark: 5–15% of project cost; best-in-class: < 3%
Source Systems: Derived from: change_events, commitment_change_orders, direct_costs, budget_modifications (all via rework codes + NLP)
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires multi-surface cost model combining financial instruments with quality record signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare rework cost % across project types and value bands to benchmark quality management effectiveness
Notes: From insight #1 (Rework Cost). CE→CCO deduplication is CRITICAL — use GREATEST(CE, CCO), never sum. Four independent cost surfaces. 40,441 projects across all surfaces. Tiered design: Tier 1 (activity only) 65%, Tier 2 (partial cost) 15%, Tier 3 (full cost) 20%.

Description: Average elapsed time from observation creation to closure — measures how quickly quality and safety findings are addressed. Formula: Closed Date − Created Date (in business days). Can be segmented by category (Quality vs. Safety), type_name (severity), and responsible vendor.
Industry Standard: Company-specific SLA targets; ISO 9001 corrective action timeliness
Common Values: Typical: 3–14 days for routine findings; > 30 days for complex corrective actions
Source Systems: Derived from: observation (created_at, closed_at, category, vendor_id)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from observation timestamps and status
Benchmarking Use: Compare observation resolution time by category and severity to benchmark responsiveness
Notes: Resolution time by vendor reveals subcontractor responsiveness to quality/safety findings. Safety observations should resolve faster than quality observations due to hazard urgency.

Description: Percentage of inspections that pass on first attempt — measures work quality at the point of formal examination. Formula: Passed Inspections / Total Inspections × 100 (or at item level: Conforming Items / Total Items). First-pass yield is a stronger quality signal than overall pass rate.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 first-pass yield; Six Sigma quality metrics
Common Values: Typical: 85–95% first-pass rate; < 80% indicates systemic quality issues
Source Systems: Derived from: inspections (result/status), inspection_items (status counts)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from structured inspection and inspection_items status fields
Benchmarking Use: Compare first-pass inspection rate across inspection types and project types
Notes: First-pass yield by inspection type reveals which quality gates catch the most deficiencies. Pre-cover and pre-pour inspections with low pass rates signal workmanship issues in hidden work.

Description: Ratio of near-miss reports to actual incidents — measures safety culture and proactive reporting. Formula: Near-Miss Count / Incident Count. Higher ratio = better safety culture (more proactive identification). Industry target: 10:1 near-miss-to-incident ratio. Low ratio suggests underreporting of near-misses, not absence of hazards.
Industry Standard: Heinrich's Safety Triangle; Bird's accident ratio; ANSI Z10 leading indicator metrics
Common Values: Industry target: 10:1 near-miss-to-incident; actual varies widely by company
Source Systems: Derived from: incident (near-miss classification), observation (safety category)
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires classifying incidents as near-miss vs. actual from incident and observation data
Benchmarking Use: Compare near-miss ratio across companies and project types to benchmark safety culture maturity
Notes: Near-miss reporting is voluntary — low ratios often indicate cultural barriers to reporting rather than safety. Companies that actively encourage near-miss reporting have better safety outcomes.

Description: Percentage of corrective actions completed by their due date — measures the effectiveness of the remediation loop. Formula: On-Time Closures / Total Corrective Actions × 100. Tracks whether identified problems actually get fixed. Overdue corrective actions indicate systemic follow-through failures.
Industry Standard: ISO 45001 §10.2; ISO 9001 §10.1; company corrective action SLAs
Common Values: Target: > 90% on-time closure; < 70% indicates follow-through breakdown
Source Systems: Derived from: incident_action (status, due_date, completion_date), action_plan_line_item (status, due_date)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from incident_action and action_plan_line_item status and date fields
Benchmarking Use: Compare corrective action closure rate across project types and companies
Notes: Closure rate by action type (immediate vs. root cause vs. preventive) reveals whether companies are doing quick fixes or addressing root causes. Cross-domain: links to Organizations (responsible party performance).
